caveats: a: The BLS occupation evidence is for NAICS 332 rather than 332410.
a: The estimate separates AI exposure from conventional robotics, although the boundary is imperfect in vision-assisted inspection and robotic welding.
rho: The NIST roadmap and NAM survey cover manufacturing generally and are not 332410 adoption measurements.
rho: Large manufacturers likely have better data and integration capacity than the target LMM firms.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and not a verified list of acquisition targets.
e: The Census questionnaire confirms product and service breadth but does not measure lens eligibility.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Internal family transfers may not result in an externally acquirable control transaction.
q: Revenue models vary between new equipment, contract manufacturing, parts, installation, and service.
q: The public-company filing reflects a much larger and more diversified supplier than the lens firms.
d5: Backlog is nominal and can reflect price, mix, acquisitions, and project timing rather than constant-quality quantity.
d5: The proxy company operates globally and across adjacent equipment categories.
o: Standardized components may shift to larger automated suppliers or imports.
o: The estimate concerns operator requirement, not whether the same incumbent firm retains the work.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Power Boiler and Heat Exchanger Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily engineered-to-order or configured equipment and components sold under project or purchase-order contracts, with repeat parts, retrofit, maintenance, repair, and installation revenue for industrial, energy, defense, data-center, and process customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal shops, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, pure distributors, and installers that do not manufacture the equipment.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers supplying custom or repeat power boilers, heat exchangers, condensers, related components, contract fabrication, installation, repair, and service to external industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 332 occupation mix, not wage-weighted tasks in 332410. I map the prevalence of welders, machinists, assemblers, press operators, and production supervisors to low exposure for hands-on fabrication, while assigning higher exposure to engineering documentation, estimating, scheduling, procurement, quality records, and administration. The broader-subsector proxy may overstate exposure if pressure-equipment work is more craft- and inspection-intensive than average.
caveats: The BLS occupation evidence is for NAICS 332 rather than 332410.
The estimate separates AI exposure from conventional robotics, although the boundary is imperfect in vision-assisted inspection and robotic welding.
a.change_evidence: A 332410-specific occupational staffing matrix joined to O*NET task-level time and wage data, plus time studies from independent boiler and exchanger shops, would materially refine the exposed share.
a.rationale: Most shop-floor cutting, forming, welding, assembly, testing, and installation remains physical. AI can materially assist quotation, design retrieval, drafting checks, production planning, purchasing, documentation, and inspection triage, but these are a minority of the wage bill.

d5.bridge: Chart Industries is a large global equipment supplier rather than a representative US LMM 332410 firm. I use its 2025 Heat Transfer Systems backlog increase from $2.097 billion to $2.141 billion, stable orders, and cited data-center offset to traditional-energy weakness as directional evidence of mixed but positive end-market demand, then temper the five-year constant-price quantity ratio for cyclicality and product-mix differences. This may overstate LMM growth if large suppliers capture data-center and LNG projects disproportionately, or understate it if subcontractors participate broadly.
caveats: Backlog is nominal and can reflect price, mix, acquisitions, and project timing rather than constant-quality quantity.
The proxy company operates globally and across adjacent equipment categories.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar shipment and order forecast at six-digit NAICS detail, split by boiler, heat exchanger, nuclear, data-center, LNG, parts, and service end markets, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Aging industrial assets and repeat demand for heat transfer, parts, retrofit, and service support durability, while data centers, LNG, nuclear, carbon capture, and energy infrastructure offer growth. Traditional energy cycles, long project timing, customer capital budgets, and substitution among technologies keep the range wide.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and not a verified list of acquisition targets.
The Census questionnaire confirms product and service breadth but does not measure lens eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 112 dataset-estimated LMM firms, including ownership, customer mix, certification status, employee depth, and captive-versus-merchant activity, would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Many independently owned specialist boiler, exchanger, component, and repair shops can meet the external-customer and transferable-operation lens. Ineligibility arises from captive units, firms dominated by owner-only expertise, large strategic manufacturers, project shells, and businesses lacking durable certifications, customer relationships, or a separable operating team.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332410 ($1,997,354,439) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332410 ($8,242,554,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $362,868 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332410); class <5: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,539,053 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $301,962 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,698,867 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $333,318 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,011,765 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $787,108 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,769,316 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,897,740 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,773,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,765,593 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $148,723,114 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,179,475 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 112

caveats: Standardized components may shift to larger automated suppliers or imports.
The estimate concerns operator requirement, not whether the same incumbent firm retains the work.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and make-versus-buy data showing insourcing, standardized modular substitution, additive manufacturing, or software-enabled self-service in current 332410 work would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Customers will still require an accountable fabricator for physical boilers, exchangers, pressure components, traceable materials, inspection, installation, and field service. Software and AI can change how the operator works, but cannot eliminate fabrication, liability-bearing release, or physical delivery of the current service basket within five years.

caveats: Revenue models vary between new equipment, contract manufacturing, parts, installation, and service.
The public-company filing reflects a much larger and more diversified supplier than the lens firms.
q.change_evidence: A sample of LMM customer contracts and bids showing pricing basis, escalation clauses, open-book provisions, rebid frequency, win-rate elasticity, and realized margin after productivity improvements would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Quoted or contracted equipment work lets an operator retain productivity benefits during a project, and engineering know-how, qualifications, lead times, and installed-base service can slow immediate repricing. Over five years, competitive bids, customer cost pressure, renegotiation, and improved industry productivity share a meaningful portion of savings with buyers.

caveats: The NIST roadmap and NAM survey cover manufacturing generally and are not 332410 adoption measurements.
Large manufacturers likely have better data and integration capacity than the target LMM firms.
rho.change_evidence: Deployment audits from LMM pressure-equipment fabricators showing use-case adoption, pre/post labor hours, exception rates, rework, and auditor or customer acceptance would materially move this range.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful in estimating, document search, scheduling, procurement, and quality-record workflows. It will be slower in design release, welding, nondestructive testing, and safety-critical decisions because heterogeneous legacy data, traceability, qualification, and accountable human review remain binding constraints.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US employer-business owners across industries, with 22% reporting such plans. I use that as the anchor, shade the base slightly downward for the gap between intention and completed qualifying control transfers, and allow an upper tail for an aging specialist-manufacturer ownership base. The proxy may overstate completed transfers because plans can lapse, or understate them because unsolicited strategic sales are not always planned.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Internal family transfers may not result in an externally acquirable control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed private-company transactions tagged to 332410 from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and specialist industrial M&A advisers, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: The national employer-owner succession signal supports a substantial but not majority five-year transfer probability. Specialized certifications, customer approvals, and owner technical dependence can impede closure even when an owner wants to exit.

principal_driver: Durable need for accountable physical fabrication combined with AI-assisted engineering and operating workflows.
principal_weakness: AI addresses only a minority of labor while safety, certification, legacy-data, and project-cycle constraints slow implementation.
